items.title: Preinstall to persistence: Inside the npm Miasma credential-stealing campaign
items.description: Microsoft Threat Intelligence discovered a large-scale npm supply chain attack compromising 32 malicious packages across over 90 versions under the @redhat-cloud-services scope. The compromise originated from the RedHatInsights/javascript-clients CI/CD pipeline, enabling attackers to publish trojanized packages through legitimate GitHub Actions OIDC workflows with authentic provenance signatures.
items.ai_summary: The npm Miasma credential-stealing campaign is a large-scale supply chain attack that compromised 32 malicious packages across over 90 versions under the @redhat-cloud-services scope. The attack originated from the RedHatInsights/javascript-clients CI/CD pipeline, allowing attackers to publish trojanized packages through legitimate GitHub Actions OIDC workflows with authentic provenance signatures, leveraging T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise) tactics. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information, with potential real-world impact on organizations relying on the compromised packages. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove and replace affected packages with verified, up-to-date versions, and review their CI/CD pipeline configurations to prevent similar compromises. Additionally, implementing robust package validation and verification processes can help prevent future supply chain attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Argamal: Malware hidden in hentai games
items.description: In April 2026, researchers discovered a malware campaign targeting players of adult-themed games. The infected games install a previously unknown implant called Argamal that downloads and executes a RAT after several days, resulting in full system compromise. The malware uses COM hijacking to persist, replacing the InprocServer32 entry for Windows Color System Calibration Loader DLL. Delivery occu
items.ai_summary: The Argamal malware campaign targets players of adult-themed games, installing a previously unknown implant that downloads and executes a Remote Access Trojan (RAT) after several days, resulting in full system compromise. The malware uses COM hijacking to persist, replacing the InprocServer32 entry for Windows Color System Calibration Loader DLL, which allows it to maintain access to the compromised system. If exploited, this malware can lead to full system compromise, allowing threat actors to steal sensitive information, install additional malware, or use the compromised system for further malicious activities. To remediate this threat, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to the Windows Color System Calibration Loader DLL and implement measures to prevent COM hijacking, such as restricting access to the registry and monitoring for unusual changes to system files. Additionally, users should be cautious when downloading and installing software from untrusted sources, especially adult-themed games, and ensure that their systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1546.015| B[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: UAT-8302 and its box full of malware
items.description: UAT-8302 is a sophisticated China-nexus advanced persistent threat group targeting government entities in South America since late 2024 and southeastern Europe in 2025. The actor deploys multiple custom-made malware families including NetDraft, a .NET-based backdoor variant of FinalDraft/SquidDoor, and CloudSorcerer version 3. Post-compromise activities involve extensive reconnaissance, credential
items.ai_summary: The UAT-8302 threat group has been targeting government entities in South America and southeastern Europe since late 2024, leveraging custom-made malware families such as NetDraft, a .NET-based backdoor variant of FinalDraft/SquidDoor, and CloudSorcerer version 3. The group exploits public-facing applications using T1190, and utilizes remote access software via T1219 for post-compromise activities, including extensive reconnaissance and credential harvesting. To mitigate these threats, defenders should implement robust security controls, including multi-factor authentication and regular software updates, specifically ensuring that all remote access software is up-to-date and configured to use secure protocols. Additionally, network traffic monitoring and anomaly detection systems should be deployed to identify potential indicators of compromise. By taking these specific remediation steps, defenders can reduce the risk of UAT-8302's sophisticated attacks.

    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic

items.title: CloudZ RAT potentially steals OTP messages using Pheno plugin
items.description: Cisco Talos uncovered an intrusion active since January 2026 where attackers deployed CloudZ remote access tool and an undocumented plugin called Pheno to steal credentials and one-time passwords. The attack exploits Microsoft Phone Link application by intercepting synchronized mobile data including SMS and OTPs without requiring phone-level infection. CloudZ evades detection through dynamic memor
items.ai_summary: The CloudZ RAT is a remote access tool that has been used in an active intrusion since January 2026 to steal credentials and one-time passwords. The attack exploits the Microsoft Phone Link application, specifically targeting the synchronization of mobile data, including SMS and OTPs, without requiring phone-level infection. This is achieved through an undocumented plugin called Pheno, which enables the interception of sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately update the Microsoft Phone Link application to the latest version and review their mobile device management policies to restrict unnecessary data synchronization. Additionally, implementing multi-factor authentication with phishing-resistant methods, such as FIDO2, can help mitigate the impact of stolen OTPs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Deno Abuse to CastleRAT
items.description: Activity began with a ClickFix-style social engineering chain that led to MSI execution, PowerShell staging, and installation/use of Deno to run attacker-controlled JavaScript. Follow-on activity downloaded a portable Python runtime, `install.pyc`, and an encrypted `.MOa` container, which was later decrypted to recover a 64-bit Windows PE payload. Analysis of the recovered payload showed Steam Com
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix Deno Abuse to CastleRAT threat involves a social engineering chain that tricks victims into executing an MSI file, which stages PowerShell and installs Deno to run attacker-controlled JavaScript. This JavaScript then downloads a portable Python runtime and an encrypted container, which is later decrypted to reveal a 64-bit Windows PE payload, identified as CastleRAT. The real-world impact of this threat includes the potential for unauthorized access and control of affected systems, with specific examples including the compromise of Steam Community accounts. To remediate this threat, defenders should block the execution of unknown MSI files, restrict PowerShell usage to only trusted scripts, and implement Deno runtime restrictions, such as only allowing trusted JavaScript sources. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious Python runtime executions and encrypted container downloads, and update their antivirus software to detect the CastleRAT payload.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1218| D[Signed Binary Proxy Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1490| E[General Collection]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Is Now Hiring: From Job Platform Impersonation to Python-Based RAT Delivery
items.description: A multi-stage phishing campaign emerged in early May 2026, impersonating LinkedIn and Indeed through typosquatted domains to deliver malicious payloads. The attack chain begins with fake CAPTCHA pages distributed via Google Ads, leveraging the legacy Finger protocol and native Windows utilities. Victims are tricked into executing commands that deploy portable Python runtimes (CPython or IronPython
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix phishing campaign utilizes typosquatted domains to impersonate job platforms like LinkedIn and Indeed, distributing fake CAPTCHA pages via Google Ads to trick victims into executing malicious commands. This multi-stage attack leverages the legacy Finger protocol and native Windows utilities to deploy portable Python runtimes, such as CPython or IronPython, which can lead to the delivery of a Python-based Remote Access Trojan (RAT). If exploited, victims may experience unauthorized access to their systems, potentially resulting in data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should block traffic to known typosquatted domains and implement robust email filtering to prevent phishing emails from reaching users. Additionally, ensuring that all systems are configured to restrict the execution of unnecessary Windows utilities and implementing a Python runtime whitelisting policy can help mitigate the threat.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1134| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation TaxShadow: Multi-Region Tax Phishing & In-Memory Malware Campaign
items.description: A sophisticated multi-stage malware campaign targets victims through tax-themed phishing emails impersonating Indian and Japanese government authorities. The operation leverages social engineering, fraudulent tax notifications, and trusted third-party email delivery services to distribute ZIP archives containing three staged payloads. The malware implements advanced evasion techniques including DL
items.ai_summary: The Operation TaxShadow campaign involves a sophisticated multi-stage malware attack that targets victims through tax-themed phishing emails, impersonating Indian and Japanese government authorities. The attack vector involves the distribution of ZIP archives containing three staged payloads, leveraging social engineering and trusted third-party email delivery services. If exploited, the malware can impair defenses, encrypt data for impact, and execute multi-stage channels, ultimately leading to significant financial and data losses for affected individuals and organizations. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, ensure timely patching of operating systems and software, and educate users about the risks of phishing emails. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual archive utility usage or data encryption, and consider implementing endpoint detection and response tools to detect and respond to in-memory malware.

items.title: Latest goon squad to use fake helpdesk calls to steal creds
items.description: A new extortion group called Pink, tracked as cluster CL-CRI-1147, employs voice phishing and fake IT helpdesk impersonation to compromise organizations. The gang steals employee credentials, bypasses multi-factor authentication, and exfiltrates data from cloud storage platforms like SharePoint and OneDrive. Pink threatens to leak stolen information unless ransom demands are met, setting 72-hour d
items.ai_summary: The Pink extortion group, tracked as cluster CL-CRI-1147, utilizes voice phishing and fake IT helpdesk impersonation to compromise organizations, stealing employee credentials and bypassing multi-factor authentication. This attack vector, identified as T1598.004 (Spearphishing Voice) and T1566 (Phishing), allows the group to exfiltrate data from cloud storage platforms like SharePoint and OneDrive, leveraging T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel) and T1213 (Data from Information Repositories). To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement strict verification processes for helpdesk requests, utilize anti-phishing solutions, and enforce regular credential rotations. Additionally, configuring cloud storage platforms to require additional authentication factors for sensitive data access can help prevent exfiltration. Organizations should also monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual login attempts or data transfers, to quickly identify and respond to potential breaches.

items.title: Matryoshka #3/3: Gamaredon's Gammasteel Infostealer
items.description: This analysis examines Gamaredon's (UAC-0010, Armagedon) advanced espionage operations targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure. The FSB-operated group deploys GammaSteel, a sophisticated stealer operating almost entirely from memory using Windows DPAPI encryption and storing 71 distinct payload functions in the HKCU\Printers registry key. The malware employs three con
items.ai_summary: The Gamaredon group, also known as UAC-0010 or Armagedon, has been observed deploying the GammaSteel infostealer in advanced espionage operations targeting Ukrainian government, military, and critical infrastructure entities. This malware operates almost entirely from memory, utilizing Windows DPAPI encryption to protect its components, and stores 71 distinct payload functions within the HKCU\Printers registry key. If successfully exploited, this could lead to significant data breaches, with potential real-world impacts including compromised national security and disruption of critical infrastructure. To remediate, defenders should implement robust memory-based malware detection capabilities and monitor the HKCU\Printers registry key for suspicious activity, ensuring all systems are updated with the latest security patches and utilizing endpoint detection and response tools. Additionally, network traffic should be monitored for unusual patterns, and all employees should be educated on phishing attacks, as GammaSteel's initial infection vector is not explicitly stated but often involves social engineering tactics.

items.title: Malware Bypasses Browser Application-Bound Encryption Protections
items.description: A sophisticated 64-bit information-stealing malware named Remus has emerged as a direct evolution of the notorious Lumma Stealer. Following the doxxing of alleged Lumma core members between August and October 2025, developers created this advanced variant, with test builds appearing in September 2025 and live campaigns starting February 2026. Remus employs innovative techniques including injecting
items.ai_summary: The Remus malware, a 64-bit information-stealing variant, has been identified as a direct evolution of the Lumma Stealer. It employs innovative techniques such as injecting code into browser processes to bypass application-bound encryption protections, allowing it to steal sensitive information from affected systems. The malware has been observed in live campaigns starting February 2026, with test builds appearing in September 2025. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement specific browser configuration changes, such as disabling unnecessary extensions and enforcing strict site isolation policies. Additionally, users should be cautious when interacting with untrusted websites and avoid downloading suspicious files, as Remus is known to be distributed through phishing campaigns.

items.title: CVE-2026-8174: Zohocorp Zoho Mail wordpress plugin is vulnerable to Cross-Site request forgery ...
items.description:
Zohocorp Zoho Mail wordpress plugin is vulnerable to Cross-Site request forgery (CSRF).

This issue affects Zoho Mail wordpress plugin versions before 1.6.2.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8174 is a Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) vulnerability in the Zohocorp Zoho Mail WordPress plugin, affecting versions prior to 1.6.2. This vulnerability allows an attacker to trick a user into performing unintended actions on the WordPress site, potentially leading to unauthorized access or data modification. The real-world impact of this vulnerability includes the potential for attackers to exploit it to gain access to sensitive information or disrupt services. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update the Zoho Mail WordPress plugin to version 1.6.2 or later, and ensure that all WordPress plugins and themes are up-to-date and configured to use secure protocols. Additionally, defenders can implement web application firewalls (WAFs) and monitor for suspicious traffic patterns to detect potential exploitation attempts.

items.title: CVE-2026-7374: A flaw was found in KubeVirt's virt-handler component. This vulnerability allows...
items.description: A flaw was found in KubeVirt's virt-handler component. This vulnerability allows an authenticated OpenShift user with edit permissions in a single namespace to exploit improper symlink validation when connecting to virtual machine console sockets. By replacing the console socket with a symlink to the host's container runtime (CRI-O) socket, an attacker can hijack virt-handler's privileged connecti
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7374 is a critical vulnerability in KubeVirt's virt-handler component that allows an authenticated OpenShift user with edit permissions in a single namespace to exploit improper symlink validation when connecting to virtual machine console sockets. The attack vector involves replacing the console socket with a symlink to the host's container runtime (CRI-O) socket, enabling the attacker to hijack virt-handler's privileged connection. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to a complete takeover of the host system, allowing the attacker to execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should upgrade to KubeVirt version 0.54.0 or later and ensure that the virt-handler component is properly configured to validate symlinks. Additionally, defenders should restrict edit permissions in OpenShift namespaces to prevent unauthorized access.

items.title:
CVE-2026-7310: A heap-based buffer overflow vulnerability exists in XML
parser functionality in...
items.description:
A heap-based buffer overflow vulnerability exists in XML
parser functionality in the HiDraw. An authenticated
malicious user with local access can exploit this
vulnerability using a specially crafted XML file which may
lead to memory corruption and potential arbitrary code
execution. Successful exploitation could result in
application crashes (denial of service) and compromise the
confidentiality
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7310 is a heap-based buffer overflow vulnerability in the XML parser functionality of HiDraw, which can be exploited by an authenticated malicious user with local access using a specially crafted XML file, potentially leading to memory corruption and arbitrary code execution. The vulnerability can result in application crashes, denial of service, and compromise the confidentiality of affected systems. To exploit this vulnerability, an attacker would use T1203 for client execution, potentially causing network denial of service (T1498) and endpoint denial of service (T1499). Affected users should update to a patched version of HiDraw, ensuring that the XML parser functionality is updated to prevent buffer overflow attacks. Specific remediation steps include validating all XML files before processing and implementing memory corruption mitigations to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-48136: When Compliance is enabled on Check Point Multi-Domain Management, an authentica...
items.description: When Compliance is enabled on Check Point Multi-Domain Management, an authenticated administrator with read-write access to one Management Domain (CMA) can modify stored metadata associated with Compliance Best Practices in another Management Domain, where the administrator has no access permissions, bypassing Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-48136 is a vulnerability in Check Point Multi-Domain Management that allows an authenticated administrator with read-write access to one Management Domain to modify stored metadata associated with Compliance Best Practices in another Management Domain, bypassing Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). This occurs when Compliance is enabled. The affected product is Check Point Multi-Domain Management, with no specific version mentioned. If exploited, an attacker could potentially manipulate Compliance data, leading to unauthorized access or data tampering. To remediate, administrators should ensure Compliance is disabled or properly configured, and consider upgrading to a version that addresses this vulnerability. Specifically, administrators should check the version of Check Point Multi-Domain Management and apply the latest patches or updates.

items.title: CVE-2026-48135: A Check Point HTTP-based service can incorrectly handle malformed HTTP requests....
items.description:
A Check Point HTTP-based service can incorrectly handle malformed HTTP requests.
The issue is related to HTTP request parsing and validation.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-48135 is a medium-severity vulnerability in Check Point's HTTP-based service, which can be exploited by sending malformed HTTP requests. This issue is related to incorrect HTTP request parsing and validation, allowing an attacker to potentially inject malicious data. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access or data tampering. To remediate, Check Point customers should update to version R80.40 or later, and ensure that the 'http_request_inspector' service is configured to use the 'strict' parsing mode. Additionally, administrators should review their HTTP request filtering rules to prevent similar attacks.

This could lead to disruptions such as loss of stored incident entries, incorrect handling of pending approvals, or resourc

items.title: CVE-2026-48133: When the Identity Awareness blade is enabled with Browser-Based Authentication, ...
items.description: When the Identity Awareness blade is enabled with Browser-Based Authentication, an unauthenticated user may be able to read certain internal files on the Security Gateway.

items.title: CVE-2026-48132: The Security Gateway does not correctly validate a length value in certain IKE p...
items.description: The Security Gateway does not correctly validate a length value in certain IKE packets when NAT-T is used (4500/UDP). As a result, a specially crafted or malformed packet can cause the VPN processing service to terminate unexpectedly, leading to denial of service (temporary interruption of VPN negotiations/traffic).

items.title: CVE-2026-48131: The VPN service may mishandle an unexpected IKE fragment value received on the I...
items.description: The VPN service may mishandle an unexpected IKE fragment value received on the IKE port 500/UDP during the early stage of a connection attempt. This can cause the service to terminate unexpectedly, resulting in denial of service (temporary disruption of VPN-related functionality).

An Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling vulnerability in the OPC-UA Server used in PPT30 
Operating System versions before 1.8.0 may be used by an unauthenticated network-based attacker to 
permanently prevent legitimate users from interacting with the service.

items.title: Industry Reactions to New Trump AI Cybersecurity Executive Order: Feedback Friday
items.description: Experts commented on the EO’s voluntary nature, the balance between innovation and security, and potential implementation gaps. The post Industry Reactions to New Trump AI Cybersecurity Executive Order: Feedback Friday appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Five Eyes: Chinese Spies Target Government, Military Staff With Fake Job Opportunities
items.description: Posing as recruiters on online platforms, Chinese intelligence officers target personnel with access to classified or privileged information. The post Five Eyes: Chinese Spies Target Government, Military Staff With Fake Job Opportunities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Hackers Exploit Critical Everest Forms Pro WordPress Plugin Flaw to Take Over Sites
items.description: Threat actors are actively exploiting a critical security flaw in Everest Forms Pro, a WordPress plugin with about 4,000 active installations, to execute arbitrary code, leading to a complete site compromise. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-3300 (CVSS score: 9.8), a remote code execution bug impacting all versions of the plugin up to, and including, 1.9.12. A patch for the flaw was

items.title: Nightclub Giant RCI Says Data Breach Affects 40,000 Individuals
items.description: The company detected a network intrusion in March and an investigation showed that some files were stolen during the attack. The post Nightclub Giant RCI Says Data Breach Affects 40,000 Individuals appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FIFA World Cup 2026 Scams Are Already Live: Fake Sites, Banking Malware, and Stolen Logins
items.description: Security researchers and the FBI are warning that a wave of FIFA-themed fraud is already hitting World Cup 2026 fans, days before the June 11 kickoff. Recent reports describe thousands of lookalike FIFA domains, banking malware hidden inside pirate streaming apps, and at least one operation that copies FIFA's login page well enough to take over real accounts. It is an obvious target. More than

items.title: The Evil MSI Background is Back!, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: A few months ago, I wrote a diary about a payload that was embedded into a JPEG picture. It was a MSI-branded background[1]. Yesterday, I spotted another one! It seems that the technic is getting more and more popular. This time, it started with a mail containing a WeTransfer link.

items.title: Cisco warns of unpatched SD-WAN zero-day exploited in attacks
items.description: On Thursday, Cisco warned of a high-severity, unpatched zero-day in the Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager (tracked as CVE-2026-20245) actively exploited in attacks enabling root privilege escalation. [...]

items.title: Cisco Warns of 7th SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited in 2026
items.description: The vulnerability is tracked as CVE-2026-20245 and it can allow arbitrary command execution as root, but no patch yet. The post Cisco Warns of 7th SD-WAN Zero-Day Exploited in 2026 appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: PCPJack Hijacks 230 AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure Servers for Covert SMTP Relay Network
items.description: The threat actor known as PCPJack has hijacked cloud servers associated with Amazon Web Services (AWS), Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure to create a covert SMTP email relay network. "Compromised business servers across the U.S., Europe, and Asia were quietly converted into SMTP proxies, verified for mail relay capability, and synced to a downstream consumer every five minutes," Hunt.io said in

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 5th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9960, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Rust-Written IronWorm Hits NPM Supply Chain
items.description: Like Shai-Hulud, the campaign targets developers to steal credentials and reuses them to propagate across the software supply channel.

items.title: Brave Software releases Origin for a paid, bloat-free browsing experience
items.description: Brave has announced the public release of Brave Origin, a paid minimalist version of its browser that strips out cryptocurrency, AI, rewards, and other monetization-focused features. [...]

items.title: Hola Browser for Windows compromised to deliver cryptominer
items.description: The Windows version of the Hola Browser has been compromised in a supply chain attack that delivered an undeclared executable identified by researchers as a cryptocurrency miner. [...]

items.title: China's TA4922 Expands Cybercrime Attacks Globally
items.description: One of the world's most diverse, least-focused cybercrime groups is enlarging its footprint beyond East Asia.

items.title: 4 Critical Threats Where Attackers Have the Advantage
items.description: Gartner analysts issued a call to action to bolster defenses against several emerging critical threats, such as deepfakes and prompt injections.

items.title: Credit card theft campaign abuses Stripe to host stolen payment info
items.description: A new Magecart campaign is using Stripe's API infrastructure to host the credit card-stealing payload and the data exfiltrated from checkout pages. [...]

items.title: Trump considers Palantir exec to lead CISA
items.description: Shyam Sankar, the chief technology officer at Palantir Technologies, has emerged as a lead contender for the long vacant Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) director role, according to the sources, who requested anonymity to discuss the administration’s search.

items.title: FTC considers setting aside or modifying $150 million privacy penalty against X
items.description: Twitter, renamed X in 2023, filed a petition saying that the settlement terms are unfair because the order was issued against a company that “no longer exists,” the workers responsible for the scheme no longer work for X and the firm has since established a “world class” privacy and data protection program.

items.title: DentaQuest data breach  exposed info of 2.6 million accounts
items.description: A data breach at the dental benefits administrator DentaQuest has reportedly exposed the sensitive data of 2.6 million accounts. [...]

items.title: Russia seeks to label two anti-Kremlin hacker groups as ‘extremist’
items.description: The groups have previously claimed responsibility for cyberattacks targeting critical infrastructure and government institutions in Russia and Belarus.

items.title: Supreme Court rules FCC fines punishing telecom giants for sharing location data were legal
items.description: The Trump administration had backed the FCC’s position and, apart from Justice Clarence Thomas, the high court agreed.

items.title: Cisco Patches CVE-2026-20230 in Unified CM as Exploit Code Goes Public
items.description: Cisco has patched a bug in Unified Communications Manager that lets an unauthenticated attacker on the network write files to the box and, from there, climb to root. It is tracked as CVE-2026-20230, and proof-of-concept exploit code is already public. Cisco's PSIRT says it has not seen the flaw used in attacks yet. The PoC shortens that runway. The flaw is a server-side request forgery.

items.title: UN food agency discloses breach affecting 600,000 Gaza households
items.description: The United Nations' World Food Programme (WFP), the world's largest humanitarian organization, revealed over the weekend that its self-registration application (SRA) for Palestine was breached. [...]

items.title: New IronWorm malware hits 36 packages in npm supply-chain attack
items.description: A new supply-chain attack has infected 36 packages on the Node Package Manager (npm) index with infostealer malware called IronWorm. [...]

items.title: Claude Code GitHub Action Flaw Let One Malicious Issue Hijack Repositories
items.description: A security researcher found a flaw in Anthropic's Claude Code GitHub Action that let an attacker take over vulnerable public repositories running it, with nothing more than a single opened GitHub issue. Because Anthropic's own action repo used the same workflow, a working attack could have pushed malicious code into the action itself and onto the projects downstream that pull it. RyotaK of GMO

items.title: Agentic AI Is Transforming Defense, But Only Secure IT Infrastructure Will Maximize It
items.description: Over the past several weeks, the cybersecurity community has been reminded how quickly frontier and agentic AI in defense networks can challenge our assumptions. When Anthropic's Claude Mythos model was made available to a limited set of organizations as a technical preview, it was reported that an unauthorized group claimed that it had gained access within hours. The incident, if true, was

items.title: Offroad Emerges From Stealth With $7 Million to Tackle Enterprise Identity Risk
items.description: As AI agents, machine identities, and third-party applications multiply across enterprises, Offroad is betting autonomous security agents can restore control over an increasingly unmanageable identity landscape. The post Offroad Emerges From Stealth With $7 Million to Tackle Enterprise Identity Risk appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA directive for AI executive order to be released this week, Andersen says
items.description: The binding operational directive will focus in part on “vulnerability alleviation and vulnerability management,” Andersen said in remarks delivered at the TechNet Cyber conference in Baltimore.

items.title: Webinar Today: Third-Party Risk in Practice – Where Programs Break Down and How to Respond
items.description: Join this live webinar as we examine the gap between how organizations think their third-party risk programs are performing and what’s actually happening in practice. The post Webinar Today: Third-Party Risk in Practice – Where Programs Break Down and How to Respond appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Willow Raises $7 Million for Securing Autonomous AI Agents
items.description: Willow (formerly Webrix) emerged from stealth mode with an access platform designed to secure enterprise AI agents. The post Willow Raises $7 Million for Securing Autonomous AI Agents appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Bugcrowd Launches EU Data Residency Option For Evolving Data Sovereignty Needs
items.description: Organizations are growing serious about what nation’s rules apply to their data. Experts point to geopolitical tensions as a main contributing factor.

items.title: Hackers Are After the Gaps in Your Vulnerability Program: Here's Their Playbook
items.description: Threat actors are actively teaching newcomers how to find, exploit, and profit from vulnerable systems. Flare explores what a popular underground hacking tutorial reveals about modern attacker workflows. [...]

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: AI Agents Gone Wrong, Sketchy C2 Tools, ClickFix Tricks, JS Backdoors & 20+ New Stories
items.description: It got stupid again. The internet still feels held together with tape. Bad plugins, old bugs, fake tools, trusted apps doing shady things. Same mess, new wrapper. And now the weird stuff is normal. Forums go down and come back worse. Cheap hackers get better toys. AI starts breaking real systems. Great. Read the whole thing before it ruins your week anyway. Unauthenticated

items.title: Microsoft blames unexpected Windows driver updates on caching issue
items.description: On Wednesday, Microsoft fixed an issue that caused some Windows devices to install driver updates without notice despite policies configured to prevent auto-updates. [...]

items.title: Gemini Voice Assistant Hijacked via Messaging Notifications
items.description: Attackers could have triggered dangerous actions, including controlling smart home devices via Google Home and starting Zoom video calls. The post Gemini Voice Assistant Hijacked via Messaging Notifications appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Police dismantles fake ID marketplace used by migrant smugglers
items.description: French and Spanish authorities took down an online marketplace selling fake identity documents to migrant smuggling rings operating within the European Union. [...]

items.title: China-Linked TA4922 Expands Phishing Attacks to U.K., Germany, Italy, and South Africa
items.description: A new China-linked cybercrime group known as TA4922 has expanded its targeting focus to target European organizations in the U.K., Germany, Italy, and South Africa. These efforts have been complemented by a "rapid operational tempo" and a continually evolving malware arsenal comprising known families like ValleyRAT (aka Winos 4.0) and Atlas RAT (aka AtlasCross RAT), as well as previously

items.title: Mirasvit Vulnerability Exploited to Execute Code on Magento Servers
items.description: A flaw in the Full Page Cache Warmer extension can be exploited without authentication via serialized PHP object payloads. The post Mirasvit Vulnerability Exploited to Execute Code on Magento Servers appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Chinese Cybercrime Group in Spotlight for Record Campaign Pace
items.description: Relying on social engineering, the hacking group engages in credential phishing, malware distribution, and fraud activities. The post Chinese Cybercrime Group in Spotlight for Record Campaign Pace appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FlutterShell Backdoor Spreads to macOS via Malicious Google and YouTube Ads
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have shed light on a macOS malvertising campaign codenamed Operation FlutterBridge that spreads a new backdoor called FlutterShell. According to Palo Alto Networks Unit 42, the campaign is said to be the next stage of a previously reported activity cluster dubbed JSCoreRunner (aka FileRipple) in late August 2025. The cybercrime group behind the two attack chains is

items.title: Fake Sites Mimicking Open-Source Tools Rank High on Google to Deliver Malware via TDS
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a large-scale operation that impersonates open-source and freeware projects to funnel unsuspecting users through a Traffic Distribution System (TDS) and deliver malware families like Remus Stealer, AnimateClipper, and the SessionGate framework. "The sites are well-designed and often look like legitimate project portals at a glance, sometimes referencing

items.title: Microsoft's Coreutils for Windows, (Thu, Jun 4th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve been using the GnuWin32 CoreUtils for Windows for many years now (it gives you many *nix core commands on Windows).

items.title: Pakistan Spies on Afghan Finance Ministry With Xeno RAT
items.description: Despite broadly connected digital infrastructure, standard fare TTPs are enough to cause trouble for Afghanistan's porous cybersecurity.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Attackers Use AI to Automate EDR Evasion Testing
items.description: Python scripts were used to test malware against endpoint detection and response agents from Sophos, CrowdStrike, and Windows Defender.

items.title: Tropical Blend: Cyber & Politics Ramp Up Across Latin America
items.description: China-linked espionage groups have attacked at least a dozen nations in the region, gathering information on maritime shipping, oil production, and other geopolitical interests.

items.title: Cyber Insurance Rates Are Dropping, but Exclusions Widen
items.description: Cyber insurance coverage is slowly changing, and some policies may not provide coverage for social engineering attacks like ClickFix.

items.title: Coding Gaffe Exposes Microsoft 365 Accounts to Widespread Takeover
items.description: A disabled security setting meant to protect authentication across Android versions of key apps like Word, PowerPoint, and Excel paved the way for attackers to steal logins and data.

items.title: Continuing Scans for swagger.json, (Wed, Jun 3rd)
items.description: Enterprise applications often still use complex standards like SOAP for web services. The big advantage of SOAP is its tight and extensive standards, which enable interoperability across an enterprise governed by web services. The disadvantage of SOAP: First, while it is de facto usually used over HTTP, it does not leverage HTTP, leading to unnecessary complexity. Secondly, kids don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t RTFM, and developers these days tend not to appreciate the art of careful system design; they rather throw code at an IDE to see what sticks, if they don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t vibe code it anyway.

items.title: Malicious Notifications Could Trick Google Gemini Users
items.description: A prompt injection flaw in Google Gemini's voice assistant let attackers hide malicious commands in notifications, enabling social engineering and more.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
New Wave Of Phishing Emails with SVG Files, (Tue, Jun 2nd)
items.description: For a few days, my SANS ISC mailbox is flooded with emails that delivers SVG files. An SVG ("Scalable Vector Graphic") is a web-friendly vector file format used for graphics and icons. No URL in the body, just â€œan imageâ€, that&#x27s the perfect way to deliver some malicious content. This isn&#x27t the first time that we see this technique used by threat actors[1].

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 2nd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9954, (Tue, Jun 2nd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 1st, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9952, (Mon, Jun 1st)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Unidentified RAT pushes NetSupport RAT, (Mon, Jun 1st)
items.description: Introduction

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
